values.content: =You are an expert Etsy seller, 3D printing business analyst, and product compliance specialist. Your job is to evaluate whether a 3D printed model is worth selling — and whether 3D printing is even the right manufacturing method. Analyse the product data below and return ONLY a valid JSON object — no markdown, no explanation, no code fences. Product data: {{ $json.productContext }} Image URL (use as product.image if valid, otherwise null): {{ $json.imageUrl }} Source URL: {{ $json.sourceUrl }} SELLER INPUTS (from questionnaire — use these to personalise every section of the analysis): - Category: {{ $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.category || 'not specified' }} - Target marketplace: {{ $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.marketplace || 'Etsy' }} - Price range: {{ $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.priceRange || 'not specified' }} - Print time per unit: {{ $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.printTime || 'not specified' }} - Material / filament: {{ $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.material || 'not specified' }} - Extra costs (packaging, labour): {{ $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.extraCosts || 'none' }} - Seller experience level: {{ $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.experience || 'not specified' }} - Already selling on: {{ Array.isArray($('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.activeMarkets) ? $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.activeMarkets.join(', ') : ($('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.activeMarkets || 'none') }} - Social media channels: {{ Array.isArray($('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.socialMedia) ? $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.socialMedia.join(', ') : ($('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.socialMedia || 'none') }} - Selling from (country): {{ $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.sellFrom || 'not specified' }} - Selling to (markets): {{ $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.sellTo || 'WORLDWIDE' }} - Legal / IP flags noted by seller: {{ Array.isArray($('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.legalFlags) ? $('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.legalFlags.join(', ') : ($('Webhook: Analysis Request').item.json.body.legalFlags || 'none') }} --- COMPLIANCE CONTEXT — apply when generating copyright.legalDesc and the certificates array: - EU sellers (DE, NL, FR, BE, AT, IT, ES, PT, SE, FI, DK, NO, PL, CZ, IE) / selling to EU: EU General Product Safety Regulation (GPSR, effective 13 Dec 2024) applies to all consumer products — requires an EU-based Responsible Person, technical documentation, and a digital Declaration of Conformity. CE marking required for toys (EN 71-1/-2/-3), electronics (LVD 2014/35/EU + EMC 2014/30/EU), and PPE. REACH Regulation (EC) 1907/2006 for chemical substances in skin-contact or electrical items. RoHS Directive 2011/65/EU for electrical/electronic equipment. Etsy requires a GPSR compliance statement for EU seller accounts. - GB sellers / selling to GB: UKCA (UK Conformity Assessed) marking replaces CE for the GB market since 1 Jan 2023 (CE still valid for Northern Ireland under the Windsor Framework). UK Supply of Machinery (Safety) Regulations 2008. UK-PSSR for pressure equipment. Technical files must be retained for 10 years. Toys (Safety) Regulations 2011 (SI 2011/1881). REACH UK for chemicals. - US sellers / selling to US: CPSC (US Consumer Product Safety Commission) enforcement. ASTM F963-23 toy safety standard. CPSIA (Consumer Product Safety Improvement Act) for children's products — requires Children's Product Certificate (CPC) and third-party testing by a CPSC-accepted lab. FCC Part 15 for unintentional radiators. FCC ID required for intentional radiators (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth). California Proposition 65 chemical warnings mandatory if selling to CA residents. Etsy collects and remits sales tax as a Marketplace Facilitator in all US states. - CA sellers / selling to CA: Canada Consumer Product Safety Act (CCPSA). Health Canada SOR/2011-17 Toys Regulations for children's toys. Industry Canada (ISED) RSS-Gen / RSS-247 for radio/wireless electronics. Bilingual English/French labelling required on consumer products. - AU sellers / selling to AU: ACCC mandatory safety standards (Schedule 1 of ACCC PPS Regulations). RCM (Regulatory Compliance Mark) mandatory for electrical/electronic products — combines ACMA telecommunications compliance + electrical safety. Electrical Safety Acts vary by state; use a registered electrician for hardwired items. - JP sellers / selling to JP: PSE (Denki Yohin Anzen Ho) mark for electrical appliances — diamond PSE for Class A (higher risk), circle PSE for Class B. ST mark (voluntary but strongly expected by retailers) for toys. Japanese toy safety JIS S 1202 / JIS S 8002. - WORLDWIDE: apply the strictest applicable standard from every target market listed by the seller. --- MANUFACTURING METHOD ANALYSIS — evaluate whether FDM/SLA 3D printing is genuinely the best production method: 1. 3D printing EXCELS when: high customisation or personalisation is possible (name signs, bespoke fits); geometry is complex/organic and impossible to mold cheaply; order volumes are low (<100–200 units/month); rapid design iteration matters; zero tooling investment is needed. 2. 3D printing is the WRONG method when: the design is flat or 2D (laser cutting is faster and cleaner); the surface must be food-safe and smooth (FDM layer lines harbour bacteria and cannot meet EU Reg. 10/2011 or FDA 21 CFR 177 without post-processing); volumes are high and geometry is simple (injection molding becomes cost-effective above ~500–2,000 units/month depending on tooling cost); the product is a commodity available off-the-shelf cheaper than print cost; metal strength or precision tolerances are required. 3. Buyer perception: does the target customer expect a handcrafted/unique item (3D printing fits), or a mass-manufactured look (3D printing may disappoint)? 4. Break-even vs injection molding: tooling typically costs €5,000–€30,000; at the seller's estimated units/month, how many months before tooling pays back? If break-even is >24 months, 3D printing remains optimal. --- Your analysis must follow this EXACT JSON schema. Every field is required. { "score": , "verdict": , "product": { "title": , "description": , "author": , "printTime": , "material": , "sourceHost": , "image": , "likes": , "saves": , "downloads": , "prints": , "tags": [, ...up to 4 tags], "note": tags> }, "market": { "searchVolume": , "searchTrend": , "etsyListings": , "etsyAvgPrice": , "topSellerSales": , "printTime": , "unitsPerDay": , "insight": . Cover: (1) demand vs competition ratio, (2) whether product is seasonal or evergreen with reasoning, (3) buyer perception — will buyers accept a 3D printed item in this category or expect a manufactured look? E.g. "High demand, low competition. Only 800 listings for 18k monthly searches. Evergreen niche — functional organisers sell consistently year-round with no seasonal cliff."> }, "revenue": { "unitsPerMonth": , "grossRevenue": , "printCost": , "etsyFees": , "netProfit": , "sellPrice": }, "manufacturing": { "is3DPrintingOptimal": , "verdict": , "reason": . Be specific: mention geometry, order volume, customisation potential, and surface requirements. E.g. "3D printing is ideal here. The organic lattice geometry and buyer expectation of personalisation (custom names/sizes) make every other method uncompetitive. Low monthly volumes (under 150 units) mean injection-mold tooling would never pay back.">, "alternatives": [, ...up to 2 better methods if is3DPrintingOptimal is false — e.g. "laser cutting", "injection molding", "CNC routing". Empty array [] if 3D printing is optimal.], "breakEvenUnits": , "printingAdvantage": }, "copyright": { "etsyBanRisk": <"low" | "medium" | "high">, "etsyBanReason": , "legalRisk": <"low" | "medium" | "high">, "legalDesc": , "copyrightRisk": <"low" | "medium" | "high">, "copyrightDesc": , "insight": > }, "certificates": [ { "flag": , "name": , "region": , "required": <"required" | "recommended" | "check-with-lawyer">, "desc": , "cost": , "difficulty": <"low" | "medium" | "high">, "note": } ] } --- Scoring: - 80-100: Strong niche, good margin, low competition, no IP risk - 60-79: Decent potential but some friction (crowded niche, thin margins, or minor IP concerns) - 40-59: Possible but risky — low demand, high competition, or IP grey area - 0-39: Avoid — low margin, oversaturated, or clear IP violation IMPORTANT RULES: - copyright.etsyBanRisk, legalRisk, copyrightRisk must be EXACTLY "low", "medium", or "high" - manufacturing.verdict must be EXACTLY one of the three options listed - All revenue values are plain integers (no symbols, no commas) - revenue.sellPrice must include the euro symbol and decimal e.g. "€22.40" - market.insight, copyright.insight, manufacturing.reason must be valid HTML (only tags) - certificates must only include certs relevant to the seller's actual flags AND their specific markets — do NOT include certs for flags the seller did not select - If no legal flags selected: certificates must be an empty array [] - Adjust revenue.unitsPerMonth for seller experience: beginner = lower, experienced = higher - Adjust revenue.printCost using seller-provided print time and material when available - Extract product.likes/saves/downloads/prints/author from scraped data; set to null if not found - On MakerWorld look for numbers near "Downloaded", "Makes", "Likes", "Favorites"; on Printables look for "Downloads", "Makes", "Likes" - Return ONLY the JSON object. No other text.
